title: Travel Agencies

narrative.executive_view: Travel agencies offer real but contested AI operating leverage. The best lower-middle-market targets are managed TMCs, host agencies and complex-travel specialists whose books survive the founder; they can automate research, itinerary construction, servicing, commission reconciliation and routine communications. The agency channel is not presently collapsing: ARC reported a record $99.2 billion of U.S. agency air-ticket sales in 2024 [S4], while Phocuswright says the channel continued to outpace the broader travel market in 2025 [S10]. Yet consumer agentic booking is a credible bypass threat, so the underwriting case is conditional on transferable client relationships, fee income, corporate controls or a defensible complexity niche rather than on commodity booking volume.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI reaches a large share of the office workflow but not every unit of advisor value. Travel-advisor research, first-draft itineraries, fare and hotel comparisons, confirmations, schedule changes, client email, marketing and commission reconciliation can be substantially automated; 59% of surveyed advisors had used AI tools in 2025, though the leading reported use was marketing rather than end-to-end fulfillment [S2]. Relationship-led curation, supplier negotiation, exception handling, group coordination and in-destination crisis judgment remain human. The role estimate therefore treats transactional reservation and clerical work as highly replaceable, travel advising and management as partial, and physical guiding as nearly nonreplaceable.
narrative.value_capture: On an unchanged booking, labor savings can remain in the agency because revenue is generally supplier commission or override plus client fees, not a cost-plus reimbursement [S8]. Capture is weaker than that sentence suggests: suppliers can reset commission and override tiers, advisors may own the client relationship and re-affiliate, and consumer AI can shift the booking interface upstream. The air-ticket precedent is sobering: GAO found airline payments to agencies fell 57% from 1999 to 2002 as broad commissions gave way to negotiated arrangements [S9]. I therefore model moderate distribution retention and below-half competitive moat, with better outcomes for corporate policy/duty-of-care, luxury, cruise and group books than for simple leisure transactions.
narrative.adoption_timing: Tool exposure is already broad, but effective labor substitution is not. The 59% AI-use statistic includes lightweight content generation, while embedded workflow products are only now moving into confirmations, audits and itinerary production [S2]. Consumer demand is also advancing: 40% of surveyed travelers expressed interest in AI booking trip components, and Phocuswright expected meaningful bookings to move into generative interfaces [S3]. A roughly 25% current effective-adoption estimate and 2.5 years to 50% are deliberately below the tool-use headline and reflect integration, data-quality, supplier-connectivity and approval barriers.
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is a visible buyer market, but it spans very different assets. Travel Weekly described a 2025 wave including Amex GBT/CWT, Direct Travel/ATPI, Trevello/Gifted Travel Network and KHM/Levarte [S7]. For the target band, specialist broker guidance places established agencies with $3-15 million revenue at roughly 4-6x EBITDA and larger platforms at 6-8x [S8]. A 5.5x entry and 7.0x exit are estimates for a professionally managed, transferable midmarket book, not observed deal medians. Underwriting must normalize owner production, distinguish gross bookings from net commission revenue, and test whether advisor, host, accreditation and override contracts transfer.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is pressured, not assumed to melt completely. AI can become the booking intermediary for simple trips, and suppliers have a demonstrated ability to claw back distribution economics. At the same time, ARC's record agency air-ticket volume and Phocuswright's evidence that clients choose advisors for relationships, service and product knowledge rather than lowest price show a live market for intermediation [S4][S10]. Managed corporate travel retains policy, negotiated-rate, reporting and duty-of-care functions, while luxury, cruise and group travel retain coordination and exception complexity. Commodity leisure and air-only books deserve a much harsher terminal view.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The code is unusually heterogeneous: it includes online agencies and large TMCs as well as local agencies, while the target band is better represented by managed TMCs, host networks and niche specialists. Census defines 561510 as agents selling travel, tour and accommodation services and excludes tour assembly and stand-alone reservation services [S1]. The deterministic wage data omit much of the 1099 advisor population; receipts may mix gross-booking and net-commission conventions; and 2022 was a recovery year. Multiples are broker guidance rather than a disclosed transaction sample. The investment decision should be conditioned on cohort-level retention, advisor contracts, fee penetration, supplier concentration and a clean net-revenue bridge. No reviewed source documents a shortage of buyers or successors for travel-agency ownership, so factor B does not claim a succession shortage.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.what_it_supports: Exact 561510 scope: establishments acting as agents selling travel, tour and accommodation services; tour operators and stand-alone reservation services are separate industries.

sources.title: Attitudes toward technology: Travel Industry Survey 2025
sources.what_it_supports: 59% of surveyed travel advisors had used AI tools in 2025; marketing content was the most common owner/manager use, with interest also in AI-created itineraries.

sources.title: 5 ways autonomous AI agents will redefine travel
sources.what_it_supports: 40% traveler interest in AI booking trip components and the expectation that some booking intent will shift into generative interfaces.

sources.title: U.S. Travel Agency Sales Total $99.2 Billion in 2024
sources.what_it_supports: ARC-accredited U.S. agency air-ticket sales reached a record $99.2 billion in 2024, up 4%, across 10,305 reporting locations including retail, corporate and online agencies.

sources.title: Travel Industry Survey 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Among 1,562 unweighted advisor respondents, 56% were older than 55, 71% identified as owners or managers, and 67% were home-based independent contractors/agencies.

sources.title: 2025: A Year in Review
sources.what_it_supports: A 2025 travel-agency consolidation wave and four named acquiring groups across TMC and host-agency tiers.

sources.title: Sell Your Travel Agency Business: Valuation and Buyer Guide
sources.what_it_supports: Specialist-broker guidance on commission/override/client-fee economics, transferability risks, and EBITDA ranges of 4-6x for $3-15 million revenue agencies and 6-8x above $15 million revenue.

sources.title: Airline Ticketing: Impact of Changes in the Airline Ticket Distribution Industry
sources.what_it_supports: Historical analogue: airline payments to travel agencies fell 57% from 1999 to 2002 as broad commissions were replaced by selective negotiated arrangements.

sources.title: Agent advantage: Who's booking with travel advisors?
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 U.S. agency channel strength and survey evidence that clients choose advisors principally for relationships, service and product knowledge rather than lowest price.

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $7,729.2M / sectoral output (T30) $36,821.3M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Reservation and Transportation Ticket Agents and Travel Clerks
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $458,147 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561510); class <5: 6,236 firms, avg revenue/firm $469,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,480 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 689 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,063,269 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $322,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 253 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,174,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,331 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 202 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,264,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,293,376 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,659,729 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,424,248 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,919,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $87,470,889 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 250
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Role-weighted task judgment for the employer tier: reservation servicing, customer support, document handling, marketing operations and commission reconciliation are highly automatable; advisor research and itinerary construction are partial; relationship judgment and physical guiding persist.
ai_replaceable_share.plausible_range: 0.43-0.64 of the supplied W-2 wage mix
caveats: The 4-digit OEWS mix includes OTAs and other 5615 employers, so the software-developer share overstates many midmarket agencies.
The 37.87% residual contains mixed occupations; treating it as predominantly administrative is a material judgment.
Independent-contractor advisors are outside the W-2 wage weights, so this is not a share of total economic labor used by host agencies.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research, comparison, itinerary drafting and booking execution automate substantially, while complex-trip curation, trust and exception judgment persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Reservation and Transportation Ticket Agents and Travel Clerks
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Ticketing, queue handling, schedule changes and routine reservation fulfillment are structured digital workflows.
Routine status, policy and servicing contacts automate; escalations and distressed travelers retain a human layer.
Reporting, forecasting and workflow management automate, but supplier negotiation, account leadership and people management remain.
In-person guiding is physical and experiential; only preparation and administrative tasks automate.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead qualification, proposal drafting and follow-up automate, while relationship-led corporate selling persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Content, campaign operations and first-pass analysis are strong AI use cases; positioning and accountability remain human.
Coding assistance raises output but architecture, integration, security and ownership remain; this role is concentrated in large platforms.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Queue allocation and quality monitoring automate more than coaching and exception supervision.
Document handling, invoicing and data entry are highly automatable, with a residual exception workload.
The large unlisted tail is judged to contain substantial back-office and commission-administration work, but also relationship and specialist roles.

pi_dist.rationale: Agencies retain savings on existing commissions and fees, but suppliers and host networks can alter overrides or splits and consumer AI can redirect bookings; the airline commission reset is a concrete precedent.
caveats: No representative source measures pass-through of AI savings for U.S. midmarket agencies; this is a mechanism-based estimate.

pi_moat.rationale: Repeat-client books, corporate contracts, advisor teams, accreditation and complex niches provide moat, but simple booking is easy to bypass and many relationships attach to individual advisors.
caveats: Moat varies sharply between corporate/luxury/group specialists and commodity leisure agencies.

t50_years.rationale: AI tool use is already common but effective end-to-end workflow adoption remains below half; vendor integration, supplier connectivity, accuracy controls and customer approval should permit a roughly two-to-three-year crossing.
caveats: Tool-use surveys are not measurements of labor-displacing effective adoption, so the bridge to t50 is uncertain.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The 59% headline measures any AI use and is led by marketing; I estimate that about one-quarter of the target population has effective use across score-relevant operating workflows.
caveats: The 1,562-response survey was unweighted and covers advisors across agency sizes, not only $1-10 million EBITDA operators.

historical_analogs.value: Online self-booking and the 1995-2002 airline commission reset eliminated commodity intermediation economics but left corporate, complex-leisure and service-fee models; NDC adoption is a slower adjacent workflow analogue.
historical_analogs.rationale: The prior channel shock directly demonstrates supplier capture and agency adaptation, while NDC shows that integration-heavy operating changes diffuse over years.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: A repeat shock could range from modest commission pressure to broad removal of commodity leisure intermediation.
caveats: Generative agents combine consumer-interface and labor-automation effects, so the internet-era analogue is directional rather than identical.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: The survey reports 56% of advisors older than 55 and 71% identifying as owners or managers; 40% over 60 is a conservative overlap estimate for owner-led agencies.
caveats: The survey does not publish the owner-by-age cross-tab, uses a 55+ threshold and is not statistically weighted; this is not an observed owner-60+ rate.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reviewed source specifically documents a shortage of buyers or successors for travel-agency ownership. Advisor labor scarcity is a different mechanism and is not used for factor B, so the documented-succession-shortage boolean is false.
caveats: Absence of qualifying ownership-succession evidence does not prove succession is easy; it means the favorable factor-B boolean is not awarded.

active_consolidators.rationale: Travel Weekly documents four distinct acquiring groups in 2025 across TMC and host-agency transactions; this is a defensible floor for active consolidators adjacent to the target band.
caveats: Two transactions involve very large TMCs and are not direct $1-10 million EBITDA comparables.

buy_mult.rationale: A 5.5x normalized EBITDA entry sits within specialist guidance for managed $3-15 million revenue agencies and below the larger-platform range, appropriate for a transferable midmarket book.
caveats: Broker guidance is not a disclosed transaction dataset; agencies often quote SDE or commission multiples and gross-versus-net revenue can change the apparent size band.

exit_mult.rationale: A scaled, manager-run platform with diversified advisors, recurring corporate or niche volume and transferable overrides could reach the cited 6-8x larger-platform range; 7.0x is the midpoint, not guaranteed expansion.
caveats: Exit assumes the platform crosses a quality and scale threshold; AI disintermediation or advisor churn could instead compress the multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Supplier commissions and volume overrides plus client planning, ticketing or service fees; air base commissions have largely been replaced by negotiated arrangements, and host agencies often retain a split.
pricing_structure.rationale: The mixed supplier-paid and client-paid structure means operating savings can initially accrue to the agency, while upstream suppliers and hosts retain contractual power over a material revenue share.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: From commission-dominant leisure books to fee-heavy corporate/complex-travel models
caveats: Revenue presentation varies between gross bookings and net commissions, so peer margins and multiples require normalization.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role-weighted replaceable share represents mature task capacity; a 2.5-year t50 and 25% current effective adoption keep near-term realization materially below the mature uplift.

terminal_value.justification: Complex leisure and managed corporate demand survive, but consumer agentic booking and supplier commission power can bypass or reprice commodity agency intermediation.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: All material proxies are disclosed: advisor surveys proxy for target-firm adoption and owner age; broker ranges proxy for private deal multiples; large-TMC deals proxy for buyer activity. Advisor labor scarcity is not used as an ownership-succession proxy.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI savings are partly retained on unchanged commissions and fees, but modeled capture is reduced for supplier commission resets, host splits, advisor mobility and consumer channel bypass; terminal pressure is assessed separately.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic employer dataset omits much of the 1099 advisor workforce, uses a 4-digit OEWS role mix containing platform businesses, and relies on 2022 receipts/labor data from the travel-recovery period; receipts may mix gross and net conventions.
sensitivity: If contractor labor were included economically, labor share and the amount of advisor work exposed to AI could be materially higher than the W-2 screen shows.
If the target resembles a human-led niche agency rather than an OTA/TMC, remove much of the software-developer weight and reduce role replaceability.
Normalize every target to net commission and fee revenue before applying EBITDA margins or multiples.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Professionally managed mid-size TMCs, host agencies and complexity-led leisure specialists within the $1-10 million EBITDA band
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census 561510 covers agents selling travel, tours and accommodation; the target-band firm classes point away from solo advisors and mega-OTAs, while tour assembly and stand-alone reservation services are outside the code.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves adoption factor A from about 6.7 to 2.5.
Materially changes capture factor C because commodity and complex niches have opposite channel power.
Changes mature labor-cost uplift and V, especially because the residual wage share is large.
Determines whether any scale arbitrage survives AI/channel risk; could erase M if exit stays near entry.

reviewer_note: Golden attempt 2 remediates 2026-07-21_gpt-5.6-sol_v312g1_561510. The factor-B succession boolean is false because no reviewed evidence documents travel-agency ownership successor scarcity; advisor labor shortage evidence is not reused. All other score selections retain the reviewed attempt-1 thesis.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=2.5, A=4
